Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A.

Facts.Text: Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. (PAL) is a Panamanian company incorporated in 1958.

By some time after June 1959, North Enterprises Limited (NEL), formerly Lasala Investments Limited, held much of the wealth generated by Robert Perez De La Sala Sr, including interests connected to the De La Sala family shipping business.

By 1967, following the death of Robert Perez De La Sala Sr, his widow Camila Vasquez De La Sala was the sole beneficiary of his will and the De La Sala family wealth, including interests connected to John Manners and Company Limited (Hong Kong) and North Enterprises Limited.

By 2005, after the death of Camila Vasquez De La Sala, her son Ernest Ferdinand Perez De La Sala was managing the De La Sala family business interests and assets and was treated within the family as the person in charge of those assets.
In August 2011, Ernest Ferdinand Perez De La Sala was a director of Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. and the other Plaintiff Companies.

In August 2011, Ernest instructed that US dollar deposits held in Singapore for the Plaintiff Companies be remitted to Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation's account with UBS Bank (Canada) in Vancouver, which he controlled.

By August 2011, assets valued at CAD 663,033,557.61 that had been held through the Plaintiff Companies were standing to the credit of an account at UBS Bank (Canada) in Vancouver controlled by Ernest.

On 2011-08-08, Edward De La Sala, Christina De La Sala and James Copinger-Symes passed resolutions to limit Ernest's authority to operate as sole signatory for the accounts of Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A., Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation and Dominion Corporation SA with UBS Bank (Canada) Vancouver.

On 2011-08-08, after learning of resolutions limiting his sole signatory authority over certain Plaintiff Companies' UBS Bank (Canada) accounts, Ernest complained to his sister Isabel, who then contacted Edward and Christina's father, Bobby.

Around August 2011, Edward sent an email to Ernest marked "lo siento mucho" apologising for his role in passing resolutions affecting Ernest's authority over the Plaintiff Companies' UBS Bank (Canada) accounts.

After Isabel spoke to Bobby in August 2011 about the resolutions limiting Ernest's authority over the Plaintiff Companies' UBS Bank (Canada) accounts, Edward, Christina and James reversed those resolutions.
By late 2011, Ernest had transferred assets worth a total of CAD 663,033,557.61 from accounts associated with the Plaintiff Companies into his personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) in Vancouver.

On 2011-04-27, a UBS Bank manager, Laurent Rossier, emailed Ernest stating that the balance booked at the REC Hasta La Vista account was USD 4,125,631.53 plus accrued interest and asked if Ernest wanted to transfer all funds to his personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) and close the account.

On 2011-04-29, Ernest signed a letter instructing UBS Trustees (Jersey) Ltd to transfer the net liquidation proceeds of REC Hasta La Vista Corp, after adding back USD 43,623.01 of fees, in specie to his personal account number 610.630 at UBS Bank (Canada).
As at 2011-07-01, assets valued at CAD 4,035,697.02 were standing to the credit of Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).
In early 2011, Ernest decided to revoke the REC-Hasta La Vista trust structure and instructed that the trust assets be transferred to his personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).

In 2011, UBS Trustees informed Ernest and members of ECJ that, upon revocation of the REC-Hasta La Vista trust, the remaining funds could not be transferred directly back to Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. or Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation because of UBS internal rules on beneficial ownership, and that the funds would instead be transferred to Ernest's personal account as settlor.

On or about 2005-03-04, assets belonging to Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation amounting to approximately USD 700,000 were transferred to an account at UBS AG Singapore in the name of REC Hasta La Vista Corp.

On or about 2005-03-04, assets belonging to Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A., including a USD 1,000,000 capital protected note maturing on 2007-09-17 and 1,588.184 units of O'Connor-UBS Currency Portfolio shares, were transferred to an account at UBS AG Singapore in the name of REC La Vista Corp.
On 2012-03-05, the Plaintiff Companies filed an application for an interim injunction against Ernest in connection with assets held through the Plaintiff Companies.

On 2012-03-06, one day after the writ in Suit 178 of 2012 was filed, Ernest attempted to transfer USD 50,000,000 from his personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to Isabel, but the transfer did not complete due to banking issues.
On 2012-04-30, Ernest transferred USD 200,000 from his personal account to Isabel.
On 2012-08-09, Ernest transferred USD 250,000 from his personal account to Isabel.
On 2012-12-05, Ernest transferred USD 1,000,000 from his personal account to Isabel.
On 2013-01-08, Ernest transferred USD 300,000 from his personal account to Isabel.
On 2013-01-17, Ernest transferred USD 1,000,000 from his personal account to Isabel.

Around 2013-02-20, Ernest arranged for the proceeds of sale of 1,700,000 CapitaLand shares, with an approximate value of SGD 6,791,500 or USD 5,489,855 based on the historical average share price on that date, to be transferred to Isabel.
On 2011-09-30, assets valued at CAD 663,033,557.61 were transferred into Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) from accounts associated with the Plaintiff Companies.

From 2011-09-30 onwards, Ernest made multiple withdrawals from his personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) for personal and legal expenses, including the five transfers to Isabel totalling USD 2,750,000.

On 2013-04-01, CAD 201,430,000 (equivalent to USD 200,000,000) was transferred from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to a stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Ltd with Credit Suisse bank.

On 2017-05-31, CAD 67,522,500 (equivalent to USD 50,000,000) was transferred from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to the stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Ltd with Credit Suisse bank.

On 2017-06-06, CAD 8,818,240 (equivalent to USD 6,800,000) was transferred from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to top up the stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Ltd with Credit Suisse bank to USD 250,000,000.

On 2017-07-06, CAD 24,950 (equivalent to USD 20,000) was transferred from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to the stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Ltd with Credit Suisse bank to keep the balance above USD 250,000,000.

On 2017-02-28, CAD 236,980 was withdrawn from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) and transferred to Rajah & Tann LLP as a deposit for engagement to act on behalf of the Plaintiff Companies.

On 2018-03-29, the Plaintiff Companies sent letters to Isabel informing her of the Court of Appeal's findings in Suit 178 of 2012 regarding Ernest's handling of the Plaintiff Companies' assets and requesting that she return USD 2,750,000 that had been transferred to her by Ernest.
On 2018-04-18, the Plaintiff Companies commenced Suit No 398 of 2018 against Isabel seeking recovery of USD 2,750,000 that had been transferred to her by Ernest.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether funds totalling USD 2,750,000 transferred by Ernest from his personal UBS Bank (Canada) account to Isabel between 2012 and 2013 were drawn from assets beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies and whether those funds can be traced back to company assets for purposes of recovery from Isabel.
Party_Details.Rule: In claims involving misdirected company assets, a claimant may trace identifiable proceeds of its assets into the hands of a recipient and seek restitution or proprietary relief where: (a) company assets were disposed of by a person in control of them; (b) the recipient obtained a beneficial receipt of assets or their traceable proceeds; and (c) the recipient's state of knowledge, assessed objectively in light of the circumstances, makes it unconscionable for the recipient to retain the benefit. Tracing is an evidential process that follows the value of an asset into its substitutes and does not itself create the claim.
Party_Details.Application: Company records and Ernest's accounting affidavit as at 2011-07-01, CAD 4,035,697.02 stood in Ernest's personal UBS Bank (Canada) account and that on 2011-09-30, CAD 663,033,557.61 of assets previously held through the Plaintiff Companies were transferred into that same account. The five transfers to Isabel totalling USD 2,750,000 occurred in 2012 and 2013, after the CAD 663,033,557.61 had been credited. The Plaintiff Companies' internal documents and UBS correspondence the pre-existing CAD 4 million in Ernest's account originated from assets of Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. and Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation that had been placed into and later withdrawn from the REC-Hasta La Vista trust. On this factual pattern, all funds in Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account at the time of the transfers to Isabel were sourced from assets that had previously been held through the Plaintiff Companies, and the USD 2,750,000 transfers can be treated as traceable to those company assets.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge relied on the prior findings in Suit 178 of 2012 that the assets valued at CAD 663,033,557.61 in Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account were not beneficially owned by Ernest but were held on trust for entities connected to the De La Sala family structure, and that Ernest's transfers of those assets into his personal account were in breach of his fiduciary duties as director of the Plaintiff Companies. The court accepted James Copinger-Symes as a credible witness and rejected Ernest's and Isabel's reliability based on the earlier High Court and Court of Appeal assessments. On Issue 1, the judge held that the Plaintiff Companies' claim against Isabel did not offend the rule against double recovery because Ernest had not fully restored the CAD 663,033,557.61, only USD 250,000,000 had been paid into a stake-holding account, and there remained both an exchange-rate shortfall and alleged lost profits. The letter from Ernest's lawyers about transferring all assets from his UBS account to a JMM account was treated as hearsay and not proof of full restoration. On Issue 2, the judge examined Ernest's accounting affidavit and the schedule of withdrawals from his UBS Bank (Canada) account. The judge noted that, apart from three early withdrawals, all withdrawals, including the five transfers to Isabel, occurred after 2011-09-30 when the CAD 663,033,557.61 had been credited. The judge inferred that the USD 2,750,000 was drawn from the misappropriated CAD 663,033,557.61 rather than from the pre-existing CAD 4 million. The timing of the attempted USD 50,000,000 transfer to Isabel on 2012-03-06, one day after the writ in Suit 178 was filed and on the same day as an injunction application, was treated as evidence that Ernest was attempting to move misappropriated assets out of reach of the Plaintiff Companies. On Issue 3, the judge addressed Isabel's late contention that the USD 2,750,000 came from Ernest's pre-existing CAD 4 million and that this was Ernest's personal money. The judge held that Isabel had not pleaded this defence and that it was unfair to prevent the Plaintiff Companies from responding. The judge found that the Plaintiff Companies' pleadings, which referred broadly to their 'Assets' and to the 'Claimed Sum', were wide enough to encompass an argument that the pre-existing CAD 4 million also belonged to the Plaintiff Companies. The judge then analysed the REC-Hasta La Vista trust. Relying on Ernest's 2005 letters, UBS correspondence, and the earlier findings in Suit 178, the judge found that the trust had been funded with assets of Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation and Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A., and that the trust was an experimental structure to manage family assets rather than Ernest's personal wealth. The judge accepted James's evidence that UBS Trustees refused to remit the liquidation proceeds back to PAL or CFC because of internal rules on beneficial ownership and instead required the funds to be transferred to Ernest's personal account as settlor. The judge concluded that, despite clause 7(a) of the trust deed stating that on revocation the trust fund would vest absolutely beneficially in the settlor, the surrounding circumstances and the family's practice showed that Ernest was holding those proceeds for the Plaintiff Companies and not as his own property. The judge therefore held that the pre-existing CAD 4 million in Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account was also beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies. On limitation, the judge rejected Isabel's argument that any equitable claim to the CAD 4 million was time-barred. The judge held that the Plaintiff Companies' claim in this suit was for recovery of USD 2,750,000 from Isabel, not for equitable relief against Ernest in respect of the CAD 4 million, that the first successful transfer to Isabel occurred on 2012-04-30 or 2012-05-23, and that the writ in Suit 398 of 2018 was issued on 2018-04-18, within six years. The judge also noted that limitation had not been pleaded as a defence and that section 4 of the Limitation Act requires express pleading. Further, the judge considered that section 22(1) of the Limitation Act applied because the Plaintiff Companies were seeking to recover trust property or its proceeds from a constructive trustee in circumstances involving a fraudulent breach of trust by Ernest, so no statutory limitation period applied. On tracing, the judge held that there was no mixed fund of Ernest's personal money and trust money in the UBS Bank (Canada) account because both the pre-existing CAD 4 million and the CAD 663,033,557.61 were beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies. Accordingly, the usual presumptions about withdrawals from mixed funds, such as those in Re Hallett and Re Oatway, were not engaged. On knowing receipt (Issue 4), the judge applied the test in George Raymond Zage III v Ho Chi Kwong, requiring: (a) disposal of the plaintiff's assets in breach of fiduciary duty; (b) beneficial receipt by the defendant of assets traceable to those assets; and (c) knowledge making it unconscionable for the defendant to retain the benefit. The first two elements were satisfied by the prior findings in Suit 178 and the tracing analysis. On the knowledge element, the judge adopted the Akindele 'unconscionability' standard as endorsed in George Raymond, holding that actual knowledge was not invariably required and that the relevant state of knowledge could be acquired after receipt. The judge rejected Isabel's portrayal of herself as a purely nominal director with no awareness of the Plaintiff Companies' affairs. The judge relied on evidence that Isabel was a director of all the Plaintiff Companies except JMM, was a bank signatory for several company accounts, intervened in August 2011 by emailing Edward to demand withdrawal of resolutions limiting Ernest's authority on the basis that the money was 'his', and had been informed by ECJ about their concerns over Ernest's handling of company assets. The judge also noted that Isabel had testified in Suit 178, had initially denied receiving money from Ernest after 2005, and later conceded receiving approximately USD 14 million related to a property transaction, with subsequent discovery revealing over USD 58 million of transfers from Ernest to her between March 2012 and February 2013. The judge considered this pattern of large transfers, the failed USD 50,000,000 transfer on 2012-03-06, and the letters of 2018-03-29 from the Plaintiff Companies' lawyers as circumstances that, taken together, made it unconscionable for Isabel to retain the USD 2,750,000. The judge concluded that Isabel was liable in knowing receipt for USD 2,750,000 and that the Plaintiff Companies were entitled to judgment against her for that amount, with the understanding that any recovery from Isabel would be taken into account in the accounting and recovery process against Ernest to avoid double recovery.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation [BVI]

Facts.Text: Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation (CFC) is a company incorporated in the British Virgin Islands in 1995.
In August 2011, Ernest Ferdinand Perez De La Sala, Edward De La Sala, Christina De La Sala and James Copinger-Symes were directors of Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation.

On or about 2005-03-04, approximately USD 700,000 belonging to Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation was transferred to an account at UBS AG Singapore in the name of REC Hasta La Vista Corp as part of a trust structure.

On 2011-04-29, following the decision to revoke the REC-Hasta La Vista trust, the liquidation proceeds of REC Hasta La Vista Corp, which included assets originally contributed by Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation, were instructed to be transferred to Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).

As at 2011-07-01, assets valued at CAD 4,035,697.02, derived from the revocation of the REC-Hasta La Vista trust funded in part by Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation, were standing to the credit of Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).

In August 2011, Ernest instructed that US dollar deposits held in Singapore for Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation and other Plaintiff Companies be remitted to a Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation account with UBS Bank (Canada) in Vancouver that he controlled.
On 2011-09-30, assets valued at CAD 663,033,557.61, including assets previously held through Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation, were transferred into Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).

On 2012-04-30, Ernest transferred USD 200,000 from his personal UBS Bank (Canada) account, which contained assets sourced from Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation and other Plaintiff Companies, to Isabel.

On 2012-08-09, Ernest transferred USD 250,000 from his personal UBS Bank (Canada) account, which contained assets sourced from Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation and other Plaintiff Companies, to Isabel.

On 2012-12-05, Ernest transferred USD 1,000,000 from his personal UBS Bank (Canada) account, which contained assets sourced from Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation and other Plaintiff Companies, to Isabel.

On 2013-01-08, Ernest transferred USD 300,000 from his personal UBS Bank (Canada) account, which contained assets sourced from Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation and other Plaintiff Companies, to Isabel.

On 2013-01-17, Ernest transferred USD 1,000,000 from his personal UBS Bank (Canada) account, which contained assets sourced from Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation and other Plaintiff Companies, to Isabel.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether assets originally contributed by Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation to the REC-Hasta La Vista trust and later held in Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account formed part of the pool from which USD 2,750,000 was transferred to Isabel, and whether those transfers can be treated as involving funds beneficially owned by Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company’s assets are placed into a trust structure and later returned to an account controlled by an individual, the beneficial ownership of those assets remains with the company if the surrounding arrangements and internal understanding the individual is holding the assets for the company. Tracing principles allow the company to follow the value of those assets into subsequent transfers and to seek recovery from recipients where the transfers are connected to the misdirection of company property.
Party_Details.Application: Documentary records in 2005, approximately USD 700,000 belonging to Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation was transferred into the REC-Hasta La Vista trust at UBS AG Singapore. In 2011, upon revocation of that trust, the liquidation proceeds, including the contributions from Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation, were transferred to Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada), forming part of a balance of CAD 4,035,697.02 as at 2011-07-01. Later in 2011, additional assets valued at CAD 663,033,557.61, which included assets previously held through Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation, were also transferred into the same personal account. The five transfers to Isabel totalling USD 2,750,000 in 2012 and 2013 were made from this account after these credits. On this factual sequence, the funds transferred to Isabel were drawn from an account funded entirely by assets that had been held through the Plaintiff Companies, including Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation, so the value transferred can be treated as traceable to company assets.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge treated the contributions by Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation to the REC-Hasta La Vista trust as part of a broader pattern in which Ernest, as the family figurehead, used trust and corporate structures to hold family assets rather than his own personal wealth. Relying on Ernest's 2005 letters and UBS correspondence, the court found that the trust was funded with company assets and that its revocation in 2011 resulted in those assets being moved into Ernest's personal UBS Bank (Canada) account solely because UBS Trustees required the proceeds to be paid to the settlor. The judge accepted James Copinger-Symes's explanation that the family and company practice was to allow assets to be held temporarily in personal or nominee accounts for administrative or tax reasons, with the understanding that the assets remained company or family assets. The judge concluded that the pre-existing CAD 4 million in Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account, which included the proceeds of the REC-Hasta La Vista trust funded in part by Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation, was beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies. Combined with the later transfer of CAD 663,033,557.61 of company assets into the same account, the judge held that the entire balance from which the USD 2,750,000 was paid to Isabel consisted of assets beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies, including Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation. On that basis, the judge treated the USD 2,750,000 as traceable to assets of all the Plaintiff Companies and imposed liability on Isabel in knowing receipt for the full amount, subject to coordination with the accounting and recovery process against Ernest to prevent double recovery.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Dominion Corporation S.A.

Facts.Text: Dominion Corporation S.A. (DOM) is a Panamanian company incorporated in 1973.
Dominion Corporation S.A. is owned by Summit Finance Corporation S.A., which in turn is owned by Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A.
In August 2011, members of the De La Sala family, including Ernest, Edward, Christina and James, were directors of Dominion Corporation S.A.

By August 2011, assets held through Dominion Corporation S.A. formed part of the overall pool of assets valued at CAD 663,033,557.61 that were transferred into Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).
On 2012-04-30, Ernest transferred USD 200,000 from his personal UBS Bank (Canada) account, which contained assets sourced from the Plaintiff Companies including Dominion Corporation S.A., to Isabel.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether assets held through Dominion Corporation S.A. that were transferred into Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account formed part of the pool from which USD 2,750,000 was paid to Isabel, and whether those transfers can be treated as involving funds beneficially owned by Dominion Corporation S.A.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a holding company structure channels assets through subsidiaries and those assets are later concentrated in an account controlled by an individual, the subsidiaries may trace their beneficial interests into that account and into downstream transfers, provided the evidential trail shows that their assets were part of the aggregated pool.
Party_Details.Application: The corporate structure described in the evidence shows that Dominion Corporation S.A. is indirectly owned by Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. through Summit Finance Corporation S.A. The assets of the Plaintiff Companies, including Dominion Corporation S.A., were managed together under Ernest's control and were aggregated into a single pool valued at CAD 663,033,557.61 that was transferred into Ernest's personal UBS Bank (Canada) account on 2011-09-30. The five transfers to Isabel totalling USD 2,750,000 were made from that account in 2012 and 2013. On this factual basis, the value transferred to Isabel can be treated as traceable to the aggregated pool of assets that included assets held through Dominion Corporation S.A.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge accepted the corporate structure described in Suit 178 and in the present proceedings, under which Dominion Corporation S.A. was part of a group of companies whose assets were centrally managed by Ernest. The court treated the CAD 663,033,557.61 transferred into Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account as a consolidated pool of assets beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies, including Dominion Corporation S.A. Although the judgment did not isolate a specific quantum attributable to Dominion Corporation S.A., the judge considered that all Plaintiff Companies had a beneficial interest in the pool and that the USD 2,750,000 transferred to Isabel was drawn from that pool. On that basis, the judge imposed liability on Isabel in knowing receipt for the full USD 2,750,000 in favour of all six Plaintiff Companies collectively, with the allocation between them to be addressed in the broader accounting process arising from Suit 178.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd (JMM) is a Singapore company incorporated in 1948.
John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd is owned by Cambay Prince Steamship Co Ltd (BVI), which is owned by Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited.
In August 2011, Ernest and members of ECJ were directors of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd.

On 2013-04-01, CAD 201,430,000 (equivalent to USD 200,000,000) was transferred from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to a stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd with Credit Suisse bank.

On 2017-05-31, CAD 67,522,500 (equivalent to USD 50,000,000) was transferred from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to the stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd with Credit Suisse bank.

On 2017-06-06, CAD 8,818,240 (equivalent to USD 6,800,000) was transferred from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to the stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd with Credit Suisse bank to bring the balance to USD 250,000,000.

On 2017-07-06, CAD 24,950 (equivalent to USD 20,000) was transferred from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to the stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd with Credit Suisse bank to keep the balance above USD 250,000,000.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the use of a stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd at Credit Suisse to hold funds transferred from Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account affects the Plaintiff Companies' ability to recover USD 2,750,000 from Isabel as part of the same pool of assets.
Party_Details.Rule: The placement of misdirected assets into a stake-holding account in the name of a group company pursuant to court orders does not extinguish the originating companies' rights to trace and recover other misdirected sums from third-party recipients, provided that double recovery is avoided.
Party_Details.Application: Funds totalling CAD 227,795,690 were transferred from Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account to a stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd at Credit Suisse between 2013 and 2017 in compliance with court orders. These transfers were separate from the five transfers totalling USD 2,750,000 that Ernest made to Isabel in 2012 and 2013. The existence of the stake-holding account and the partial restoration of assets through it do not change the fact that the USD 2,750,000 was paid out of the same UBS Bank (Canada) account that held assets beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies. The Plaintiff Companies can therefore pursue recovery of the USD 2,750,000 from Isabel while coordinating that recovery with the amounts held in the stake-holding account to prevent double counting.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge considered the transfers from Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account to the stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd at Credit Suisse as part of Ernest's compliance with court orders in Suit 178. The court noted that these transfers amounted to USD 250,000,000 and that they did not fully restore the CAD 663,033,557.61 originally transferred into Ernest's UBS account, especially when exchange-rate movements and alleged lost profits were taken into account. The judge held that the existence of the stake-holding account and the partial restoration of assets through it did not bar the Plaintiff Companies from pursuing a separate claim against Isabel for USD 2,750,000, provided that any sums recovered from Isabel would be taken into account in the overall accounting to avoid double recovery. The judge therefore treated John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd as part of the group of Plaintiff Companies with a beneficial interest in the pool of assets from which the USD 2,750,000 was paid to Isabel.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Peninsula Navigation Company (Private) Limited [BVI]

Facts.Text: Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited (PEN) is a company incorporated in the British Virgin Islands in 1995.

Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited owns all the shares in Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. and also owns Straits Marine Company Private Limited and Cambay Prince Steamship Co Ltd (BVI), which in turn owns John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd.
In August 2011, members of the De La Sala family, including Ernest, Edward, Christina and James, were directors of Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited.

By August 2011, assets held through Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited and its subsidiaries formed part of the overall pool of assets valued at CAD 663,033,557.61 that were transferred into Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).

On 2012-04-30, Ernest transferred USD 200,000 from his personal UBS Bank (Canada) account, which contained assets sourced from Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited and other Plaintiff Companies, to Isabel.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether assets held through Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited and its subsidiaries that were transferred into Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account formed part of the pool from which USD 2,750,000 was paid to Isabel, and whether those transfers can be treated as involving funds beneficially owned by Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited.
Party_Details.Rule: In a group structure where a holding company owns subsidiaries whose assets are centrally managed and later aggregated into an account controlled by an individual, the holding company may trace its beneficial interest into that account and into downstream transfers, provided the evidential record shows that its group assets were part of the aggregated pool.
Party_Details.Application: Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited owns Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A., Straits Marine Company Private Limited and Cambay Prince Steamship Co Ltd (BVI), which owns John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd. The assets of these entities were managed together under Ernest's control and were aggregated into a pool valued at CAD 663,033,557.61 that was transferred into Ernest's personal UBS Bank (Canada) account on 2011-09-30. The five transfers to Isabel totalling USD 2,750,000 were made from that account in 2012 and 2013. On this factual basis, the value transferred to Isabel can be treated as traceable to the aggregated pool of assets that included assets held through Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited and its subsidiaries.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge accepted that Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited was part of the circular and group structure described in Suit 178, under which PAL, CFC, PEN, DOM, SMC and JMM were used to hold and manage the De La Sala family assets. The court treated the CAD 663,033,557.61 transferred into Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account as a consolidated pool of assets beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies, including Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited. Although the judgment did not allocate specific amounts to each company, the judge considered that all six Plaintiff Companies had a beneficial interest in the pool and that the USD 2,750,000 transferred to Isabel was drawn from that pool. The judge therefore imposed liability on Isabel in knowing receipt for the full USD 2,750,000 in favour of all six Plaintiff Companies collectively, with the internal allocation to be addressed in the broader accounting process.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Straits Marine Company Private Limited [BVI]

Facts.Text: Straits Marine Company Private Limited (SMC) is a company incorporated in the British Virgin Islands in 2008.
Straits Marine Company Private Limited is owned by Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited.
In August 2011, members of the De La Sala family, including Ernest, Edward, Christina and James, were directors of Straits Marine Company Private Limited.

By August 2011, assets held through Straits Marine Company Private Limited formed part of the overall pool of assets valued at CAD 663,033,557.61 that were transferred into Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).

On 2012-04-30, Ernest transferred USD 200,000 from his personal UBS Bank (Canada) account, which contained assets sourced from Straits Marine Company Private Limited and other Plaintiff Companies, to Isabel.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether assets held through Straits Marine Company Private Limited that were transferred into Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account formed part of the pool from which USD 2,750,000 was paid to Isabel, and whether those transfers can be treated as involving funds beneficially owned by Straits Marine Company Private Limited.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a subsidiary's assets are managed as part of a consolidated group pool and that pool is transferred into an account controlled by an individual, the subsidiary may trace its beneficial interest into that account and into subsequent transfers, provided the evidence shows that its assets were included in the pool.
Party_Details.Application: Straits Marine Company Private Limited is owned by Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited and formed part of the group of Plaintiff Companies whose assets were centrally managed by Ernest. The assets of these companies were aggregated into a pool valued at CAD 663,033,557.61 that was transferred into Ernest's personal UBS Bank (Canada) account on 2011-09-30. The five transfers to Isabel totalling USD 2,750,000 were made from that account in 2012 and 2013. On this factual basis, the value transferred to Isabel can be treated as traceable to the aggregated pool of assets that included assets held through Straits Marine Company Private Limited.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge treated Straits Marine Company Private Limited as part of the same group of Plaintiff Companies whose assets were consolidated under Ernest's control and transferred into his UBS Bank (Canada) account. The court did not distinguish between the individual Plaintiff Companies when determining the source of the USD 2,750,000 transferred to Isabel, instead treating the CAD 663,033,557.61 and the pre-existing CAD 4 million as a single pool of assets beneficially owned by the group. On that basis, the judge held that Isabel was liable in knowing receipt for USD 2,750,000 to the Plaintiff Companies collectively, including Straits Marine Company Private Limited, with the precise internal allocation to be resolved through the accounting ordered in Suit 178.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Isabel Brenda Koutsos

Facts.Text: Isabel Brenda Koutsos is a member of the De La Sala family and the sister of Ernest Ferdinand Perez De La Sala.

Isabel Brenda Koutsos was a director of Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A., Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation, Dominion Corporation S.A., Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited and Straits Marine Company Private Limited, and was not a director of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd.

On 2011-08-08, after Ernest informed her about resolutions passed by Edward, Christina and James limiting his authority over certain UBS Bank (Canada) accounts, Isabel contacted their father Bobby to discuss the situation.

In August 2011, Isabel sent an email to Edward stating that Ernest wanted all minutes and communications with UBS Vancouver regarding three companies withdrawn and that Ernest should have sole signatory of all his accounts, describing the funds as his money.
On 2012-03-06, Ernest attempted to transfer USD 50,000,000 from his personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to Isabel, but the transfer did not complete due to banking issues.
On 2012-04-30, Isabel received a transfer of USD 200,000 from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).
On 2012-08-09, Isabel received a transfer of USD 250,000 from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).
On 2012-12-05, Isabel received a transfer of USD 1,000,000 from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).
On 2013-01-08, Isabel received a transfer of USD 300,000 from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).
On 2013-01-17, Isabel received a transfer of USD 1,000,000 from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).

Around 2013-02-20, Isabel received the proceeds of sale of 1,700,000 CapitaLand shares from Ernest, with an approximate value of SGD 6,791,500 or USD 5,489,855 based on the historical average share price on that date.

On 2018-03-29, Isabel received letters from the Plaintiff Companies' lawyers informing her of the Court of Appeal's findings in Suit 178 of 2012 regarding Ernest's handling of the Plaintiff Companies' assets and requesting that she return USD 2,750,000 that had been transferred to her by Ernest.
By 2018-04-18, Isabel had not returned the USD 2,750,000 requested by the Plaintiff Companies, and Suit No 398 of 2018 was commenced against her.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Isabel's receipt and retention of USD 2,750,000 transferred by Ernest from his UBS Bank (Canada) account between 2012 and 2013 involved funds beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies and whether, given her position and knowledge, she is responsible to restore that amount to the Plaintiff Companies.
Party_Details.Rule: A recipient of funds is responsible to restore them to the original beneficial owner where: (a) the funds are traceable to a disposal of assets in of or similar management obligations; (b) the recipient obtained a beneficial receipt of those funds; and (c) the recipient's state of knowledge, assessed in light of all the circumstances, makes it unconscionable for the recipient to retain the benefit. The recipient's knowledge may be actual or arise from awareness of facts that would put an honest and reasonable person on inquiry, and may be acquired after the initial receipt.
Party_Details.Application: Bank records and Ernest's accounting affidavit Isabel received five transfers from Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account between 2012-04-30 and 2013-01-17 totalling USD 2,750,000, and that these transfers were made after Ernest had concentrated assets valued at CAD 663,033,557.61, previously held through the Plaintiff Companies, into that account. Evidence from the REC-Hasta La Vista trust arrangements indicates that the pre-existing CAD 4 million in Ernest's account also originated from assets of Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. and Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation. Isabel was a director of all the Plaintiff Companies except John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd, was a bank signatory for several company accounts, and intervened in August 2011 by emailing Edward to demand withdrawal of resolutions limiting Ernest's authority over UBS Bank (Canada) accounts, describing the funds as Ernest's money. She had been involved in the broader family dispute over control of the Plaintiff Companies' assets, had testified in Suit 178 of 2012, and received letters on 2018-03-29 from the Plaintiff Companies' lawyers informing her of the Court of Appeal's findings about Ernest's handling of company assets and requesting return of USD 2,750,000. Despite this, she did not return the funds. On these facts, the USD 2,750,000 that Isabel received is traceable to assets beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies, and her position as director, her involvement in the events of August 2011, her prior receipt of large sums from Ernest, and the subsequent letters collectively place her in a position where retaining the USD 2,750,000 is inconsistent with the standards expected of a recipient aware of these circumstances.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge found that Ernest's UBS Bank (Canada) account contained only assets beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies, consisting of the pre-existing CAD 4 million derived from the REC-Hasta La Vista trust funded by PAL and CFC, and the CAD 663,033,557.61 transferred from accounts associated with the Plaintiff Companies. The court held that the five transfers to Isabel totalling USD 2,750,000 were made from this pool after 2011-09-30 and were therefore traceable to company assets. On the knowledge element, the judge rejected Isabel's portrayal of herself as a nominal director with no involvement in the Plaintiff Companies' affairs. The court relied on evidence that Isabel was a director of all the Plaintiff Companies except JMM, was a signatory on several company bank accounts, and had actively intervened in August 2011 by emailing Edward to demand that resolutions limiting Ernest's authority over UBS Bank (Canada) accounts be withdrawn, asserting that the money was Ernest's. The judge also noted that Isabel had been closely involved in the family dispute, had testified in Suit 178, and had previously received substantial sums from Ernest, including more than USD 58 million between March 2012 and February 2013, as revealed by discovery in Suit 178. The judge considered that Isabel's initial denial in Suit 178 of receiving money from Ernest after 2005, followed by later concessions and documentary evidence of large transfers, undermined her credibility. The judge applied the Akindele unconscionability standard as endorsed in George Raymond Zage III v Ho Chi Kwong, holding that actual knowledge of the precise legal character of the funds was not required and that the relevant state of knowledge could be acquired after receipt. The letters of 2018-03-29 from the Plaintiff Companies' lawyers, which informed Isabel of the Court of Appeal's findings in Suit 178 and requested return of USD 2,750,000, were treated as putting her on clear notice that the funds she had received were being claimed as company assets. The judge concluded that, in light of Isabel's role as director, her involvement in the August 2011 events, her history of receiving large transfers from Ernest, and the explicit demand letters, it was unconscionable for her to retain the USD 2,750,000. The court therefore held Isabel liable in knowing receipt for USD 2,750,000 in favour of the Plaintiff Companies, and indicated that any recovery from her would be coordinated with the accounting and recovery process against Ernest to avoid double recovery.
